question: How to convert a Ukrainian-language audio podcast (WAV file) into a DaVinci Resolve timeline (XML) containing a configured mouth viseme sequence synced to the sound, covering: (1) Rhubarb Lip Sync internals, Preston Blair viseme set, output formats, and limitations/phonetic-mode workarounds for non-English (Ukrainian) audio; (2) Montreal Forced Aligner Ukrainian acoustic model + dictionary names, install/CLI workflow, TextGrid phone-level parsing, Ukrainian IPA inventory, and whether a Whisper transcript is needed; (3) phoneme-to-viseme mapping schemes (IPA/ARPAbet to Preston Blair or Oculus/Polly) with a concrete mapping table; (4) DaVinci Resolve timeline interchange formats (FCPXML vs FCP7 xmeml vs OTIO vs native .drt vs Resolve Python API) for placing many still-image mouth shapes at exact frame timecodes, with minimal XML examples and seconds-to-frame conversion at 24/25/30 fps.
summary: Strategy: split along the four technical pillars named in the question (Rhubarb, MFA for Ukrainian, phoneme-to-viseme mapping, and Resolve XML interchange), then add a fifth angle on the seconds-to-frame timecode math and minimal XML structure for placing many image clips, since that is the load-bearing integration detail. Each query targets the specific tool names, model identifiers, and file formats to surface high-signal docs, GitHub repos, and forum threads rather than generic tutorials.

angles.label: Rhubarb Lip Sync internals & non-English limits
angles.query: Rhubarb Lip Sync Preston Blair mouth shapes phonetic recognition mode non-English audio dialog output JSON XML
angles.rationale: Covers focus area 1: how Rhubarb works, its viseme output set, formats, and the phonetic-vs-pocketsphinx workaround for Ukrainian audio with no language model.

angles.label: Montreal Forced Aligner Ukrainian model & workflow
angles.query: Montreal Forced Aligner Ukrainian acoustic model pronunciation dictionary download mfa align TextGrid phone tier
angles.rationale: Covers focus area 2: exact MFA Ukrainian model/dictionary names, install + CLI align workflow, and phone-level TextGrid output (and the transcript requirement implying Whisper).

angles.label: Phoneme-to-viseme mapping tables
angles.query: phoneme to viseme mapping table IPA ARPAbet Preston Blair Oculus Amazon Polly viseme set conversion
angles.rationale: Covers focus area 3: concrete IPA/ARPAbet-to-viseme mapping schemes and tables across Preston Blair, Oculus, and Polly standards.

angles.label: DaVinci Resolve timeline XML interchange formats
angles.query: DaVinci Resolve import FCPXML vs FCP7 xmeml vs OTIO timeline still image clips frame timecode Python API
angles.rationale: Covers focus area 4: which interchange format Resolve accepts for placing many still images at exact frames, comparing FCPXML/xmeml/OTIO/.drt and the scripting API.

angles.label: FCPXML/xmeml image-clip structure & timecode math
angles.query: FCPXML xmeml example image clip start duration timecode frames per second 24 25 30 fps seconds to frame conversion
angles.rationale: Integration detail: minimal XML element structure for an image clip with exact in/out timing and the seconds-to-frame conversion math at 24/25/30 fps that ties the viseme timings to the timeline.

results.title: ChatOctopus/timeline — Import/export editing timelines for FCP, Premiere, Resolve, OTIO (Python)
results.snippet: Python library that generates well-formed FCPXML 1.8, FCP7 XML / xmeml v5 (Premiere, Resolve), and OTIO with frame-accurate rational time math. Directly answers focus area 4: a programmatic way to emit a Resolve-importable timeline placing many clips at exact frame timecodes. Supports 'resolve' and 'otio' export targets — ideal for generating a viseme image sequence timeline from code.

results.title: Scripting API | DaVinci Resolve Wiki
results.snippet: Documents the native Resolve Python/Lua API including MediaPool.AppendToTimeline() with clipInfo dicts (mediaPoolItem, startFrame, endFrame, recordFrame, trackIndex, mediaType). The recordFrame field positions each clip at an exact frame — the cleanest native route for dropping hundreds of still mouth-shape images at precise frames without hand-writing XML.

results.title: DaVinci Resolve Scripting API Documentation — X-Raym
results.snippet: Readable HTML rendering of Blackmagic's official scripting README. Confirms AppendToTimeline signature, recordFrame for frame-accurate placement, and how to import/add media pool items and create timelines programmatically — the recommended approach for placing many still images at computed frame positions (seconds×fps).

results.title: What Is FCPXML? XML Interchange in Video Editing — ScriptCut
results.snippet: Explains the difference between modern FCPXML and legacy FCP7 xmeml, and that FCPXML expresses timing as rational time units (e.g. frame/fps as 1001/30000s) rather than raw frame counts. Useful for understanding the seconds→timecode/time-unit conversion needed and why an app that speaks only one dialect will reject the other.

results.title: Blackmagic Forum — Scripting API: create timeline and add clips
results.snippet: Practical thread with working Lua/Python snippets showing CreateEmptyTimeline + AppendToTimeline with clipInfo (startFrame/endFrame/recordFrame/trackIndex), including notes on positioning clips and stills at specific frames. Concrete examples for the recommended scripting workflow.

results.title: A quick note on the Final Cut Pro X XML format — Digital Rebellion
results.snippet: Describes xmeml/FCPXML structure (browser items, sequences, tracks, media ordering) and that in/out points in FCPXML are expressed in time units, not frames. Background for hand-authoring a Resolve-importable XML with still-image clipitems at exact times, and for choosing xmeml vs FCPXML.

results.title: Ukrainian MFA acoustic model v3.0.0 — official MFA models docs
results.snippet: The exact named Ukrainian acoustic model. Download via `mfa model download acoustic ukrainian_mfa`. Trained on the MFA IPA phone set with the Ukrainian pronunciation dictionaries; intended for forced alignment of Ukrainian transcripts across speakers/recording conditions. Confirms the precise model name needed for the align command in focus area 2.

results.title: Ukrainian MFA dictionary v2.0.0a — official MFA models docs
results.snippet: The matching pronunciation dictionary, downloaded with `mfa model download dictionary ukrainian_mfa`. Uses the Ukrainian MFA phone set and was used to train the acoustic model. The phone set in the dictionary must match the acoustic model — directly answers dictionary availability and exact name.

results.title: MFA IPA phone set — mfa model docs
results.snippet: Documents MFA's opinionated IPA phone set used in the Ukrainian models. This is the exact phoneme inventory that appears on the phone tier of the output TextGrid, which is the input needed for the IPA-to-viseme mapping step (focus area 3).

results.title: Ukrainian MFA G2P model v3.0.0 — official MFA models docs
results.snippet: `ukrainian_mfa` G2P model (download with `mfa model download g2p ukrainian_mfa`). Generates pronunciations for out-of-vocabulary words via `mfa g2p`, so a Whisper transcript with words not in the dictionary can still be aligned — needed because MFA requires a transcript and dictionary coverage.

results.title: First steps — Montreal Forced Aligner 3.X documentation
results.snippet: Install (conda: `conda create -n aligner -c conda-forge montreal-forced-aligner`), model download commands, and the core `mfa align corpus_dir dictionary acoustic_model output_dir` workflow that produces phone-level TextGrids. Confirms a transcript per audio file is mandatory (hence Whisper for the Ukrainian podcast).

results.title: Montreal Forced Aligner — Corpus Phonetics Tutorial (Eleanor Chodroff)
results.snippet: Practical end-to-end MFA tutorial: corpus layout (paired .wav + .lab/.txt transcripts), download/validate/align commands, and opening the resulting .TextGrid in Praat to read the phone tier. Useful concrete reference for parsing phone-level TextGrid output with start/end times per phone.

results.title: Rhubarb Lip Sync — Official README (DanielSWolf/rhubarb-lip-sync)
results.snippet: Authoritative source for focus area 1. Documents the --recognizer option (pocketSphinx for English vs phonetic for non-English/Ukrainian audio), the phonetic recognizer being language-independent (recognizes individual sounds/syllables, not English words), the --dialogFile option, output formats (TSV/XML/JSON via --exportFormat), and the 6 basic (A-F) plus 3 extended (G,H,X) Preston Blair-derived mouth shapes. This is the core reference for how Rhubarb works and the phonetic workaround for Ukrainian.

results.title: GitHub - DanielSWolf/rhubarb-lip-sync (main repo)
results.snippet: Project home page. Confirms Rhubarb is a CLI tool generating 2D mouth animation from voice; six basic mouth shapes (originating from Hanna-Barbera/Preston Blair tradition) plus up to three extended shapes; CLI can output TSV/XML/JSON. States v1.x officially supports English dialog but v1.9 added basic non-English recording support via the phonetic recognizer — directly answers the Ukrainian non-English limitation question.

results.title: Plans for Rhubarb Lip Sync 2 — Issue #95
results.snippet: Maintainer's roadmap explaining the language limitation explicitly: Rhubarb 1.x only supports English dialog because pocketSphinx uses an English acoustic/language model, while Rhubarb 2's architecture will be language-agnostic. Useful context for why Ukrainian audio must rely on the phonetic recognizer and what its quality tradeoffs are.

results.title: Preston Blair phoneme series — Gary C. Martin CGI
results.snippet: The canonical Preston Blair mouth-shape reference that Rhubarb's A-F/G-H-X viseme set is based on, including the extended phoneme series. Helps map Rhubarb's letter-coded visemes to actual mouth drawings/still images needed for the DaVinci Resolve timeline, bridging focus areas 1 and 3.

results.title: Phoneme Recognition (caveat emptor) — CMUSphinx
results.snippet: Explains the internals and limitations of PocketSphinx phoneme recognition that Rhubarb's phonetic recognizer relies on: needs a phonetic language model per phoneset, default models are US-English-only and won't work for native languages like Ukrainian, and pure phoneme recognition is error-prone. Clarifies why Rhubarb's phonetic mode is approximate for Ukrainian and motivates the Montreal Forced Aligner alternative.

results.title: Before you start — CMUSphinx Open Source Speech Recognition
results.snippet: Background on the speech engine underlying Rhubarb's pocketSphinx recognizer; notes the default model is US/Canadian English and will not work for non-English languages without custom acoustic/language models, reinforcing the Ukrainian limitation and the need for the phonetic recognizer or an external aligner.

results.title: Visemes and Amazon Polly (AWS Polly Developer Guide)
results.snippet: Authoritative, complete mapping of IPA phonemes and X-SAMPA symbols to Polly's viseme set per language. Polly visemes (p, t, S, T, f, k, i, r, s, u, @, a, e, E, o, O, sil) are a well-documented, code-friendly target set. Directly usable as the phoneme-to-viseme layer for focus area 3, with per-language tables (incl. non-English) linked from here.

results.title: Phoneme and Viseme Tables for Supported Languages: English (US) - Amazon Polly
results.snippet: Concrete three-column table (IPA phoneme, X-SAMPA symbol, corresponding viseme) with example words. This is the canonical lookup table format the question asks for; the parent 'Phoneme and Viseme Tables for Supported Languages' page links other languages, useful for mapping MFA/IPA Ukrainian phones to a standard viseme set.

results.title: Viseme Reference - Oculus/Meta OVR LipSync
results.snippet: Defines the canonical 15-viseme Oculus set (sil, PP, FF, TH, DD, kk, CH, SS, nn, RR, aa, E, ih, oh, ou) with reference mouth-shape images and the phoneme groups each viseme covers (e.g. /b,p,m/ to PP). Language-agnostic by design, making it the best-documented target set for mapping Ukrainian IPA phones to mouth shapes.

results.title: Rhubarb Lip Sync README (Preston Blair mouth shapes A-H, X)
results.snippet: Defines Rhubarb's Preston Blair-based output set: six basic shapes A-F plus optional extended G, H, and X (rest/pause), with descriptions of which sounds map to each shape. Establishes the Preston Blair target vocabulary that an IPA/ARPAbet-to-viseme table must map into for a Resolve still-image mouth sequence.

results.title: Phoneme-to-viseme mappings: the good, the bad, and the ugly (arXiv)
results.snippet: Academic survey comparing many published phoneme-to-viseme mapping schemes (Jeffers, Lee, Fisher, Disney/Hazen, etc.), explaining many-to-one phoneme clustering and which groupings work best. Useful for justifying a custom IPA-to-viseme collapse and understanding why viseme counts differ (9 vs 15 vs 20+).

results.title: Visemes - VRChat Wiki
results.snippet: Practical cross-reference of the 15-viseme Oculus set as used in production avatars, with phoneme examples per viseme and notes on collapsing to fewer shapes. Helpful as a second, accessible source for the Oculus mapping when building a concrete IPA-to-mouth-shape table.

results.title: Final Cut Pro 7 XML Interchange Format (xmeml) - Element Reference (Apple)
results.snippet: Authoritative xmeml element reference. Defines exactly the elements needed to place many still-image mouth shapes: <clipitem> with <in>/<out> (source media trim) vs <start>/<end> (placement in the enclosing <sequence>), <duration>, <file> with <pathurl> (must start with file://localhost or file:///), <rate>(<timebase>/<ntsc>), and <samplecharacteristics> width/height for stills. All timing values here are integer FRAME counts, so seconds->frame is simply round(seconds * fps) at 24/25/30 fps — the core conversion that ties viseme timings to the timeline. xmeml is well supported by DaVinci Resolve import.

results.title: Demystifying Final Cut Pro XMLs (fcp.cafe)
results.snippet: Best practical explainer of modern FCPXML timing. Explains that start/offset/duration are RATIONAL seconds (64-bit numerator / 32-bit denominator), e.g. 30030/24000s, and that the <format> frameDuration encodes fps: 1001/24000s (23.976), 1/24s, 100/2500s (25 PAL), 1001/30000s (29.97). To place a viseme image at a frame boundary you must emit time as frame*frameDuration (numerator = frame_index*frameDuration_num) or Resolve throws 'item is not on an edit frame boundary'. Covers tcFormat DF/NDF and why rationals beat decimals for frame accuracy. Directly drives the seconds->frame math for both 24/25/30 fps.

results.title: cutlass - swiss army knife for generating FCPXML files (GitHub)
results.snippet: Working code reference for programmatically generating FCPXML with images placed at exact frames - essentially the target output of the viseme pipeline. Shows how to build asset/asset-clip/video elements, compute rational start/duration on frame boundaries, and produce a file importable into Resolve/FCP. Good template for emitting one image clip per viseme at its computed frame offset.

results.title: steele-fcpxml - Python library to assemble FCPXML timelines (PyPI)
results.snippet: Python library that builds an FCPXML timeline from a list of source clips with in/out points, gaps and markers, producing an .fcpxml importable directly into DaVinci Resolve with every clip pre-positioned at the correct timecode. Practical recommended path for the Python stage of the pipeline: feed it the per-viseme image filenames and frame-converted in/out times rather than hand-writing XML. Confirms FCPXML (not native .drt) as the pragmatic interchange format for scripted image placement.

results.title: ccnmtl/xmeml - concrete example XML file (GitHub)
results.snippet: A real, complete xmeml document you can copy as a minimal template: shows the full nesting of sequence > media > video > track > clipitem with file/pathurl, rate (timebase 30 / ntsc), and integer in/out/start/end/duration. Useful as a ground-truth skeleton when emitting hundreds of still-image clipitems at sequential frame timecodes.

results.title: DaVinci Resolve Python Scripting - The Complete Guide to the API (Wild Lion Media, 2026)
results.snippet: Up-to-date (2026) reference for the alternative to XML interchange: Resolve's native Python API. Covers MediaPool clip append with frame-accurate control, ImportTimelineFromFile() for FCPXML conform, and timeline operations. Relevant for the format recommendation section (FCPXML vs Resolve scripting API) when deciding whether to generate XML or drive Resolve directly to position viseme stills.

claims.claim: FCPXML is Apple's XML interchange format (.fcpxml) for Final Cut Pro that describes clips, projects, timelines, and metadata, allowing apps to exchange edits with Final Cut Pro.
claims.quote: Apple's XML interchange format for Final Cut Pro: a structured text file (.fcpxml) that describes clips, projects, timelines, and metadata so apps can exchange edits with Final Cut Pro.

claims.claim: FCPXML encodes timeline structure including clips, their source media, order and timing on the timeline, multiple video/audio lanes, transitions, effects, and markers.
claims.quote: the structure of a project: the clips and their source media, the order and timing on the timeline, multiple video and audio lanes, transitions and effects, markers, keywords, ratings, and metadata.

claims.claim: DaVinci Resolve and Premiere Pro primarily read and write the legacy Final Cut Pro 7 XML (xmeml) format rather than the modern .fcpxml format, whose support varies.
claims.quote: Premiere Pro and DaVinci Resolve have long read and written this older XML

claims.claim: Modern .fcpxml and legacy FCP7 xmeml are incompatible formats; sending modern .fcpxml to legacy-only applications will fail.
claims.quote: These are incompatible—sending modern .fcpxml to legacy-only applications will fail.

claims.claim: FCPXML expresses timing such as in/out points in time units (rational time, e.g. fractions of a second) rather than the frame units used by the legacy FCP7 XMEML format.
claims.quote: Timing information such as in and out points are expressed in time units rather than frame units

claims.claim: FCPXML and FCP7 XMEML are structurally incompatible formats and cannot be directly imported into the other application.
claims.quote: there is no way to directly import FCP 7 XMEML into Final Cut Pro X or vice versa

claims.claim: FCPXML organizes data with resources first and a single sequence containing storylines/clips in a parent-child relationship rather than the chronological browser/sequence/track structure of XMEML.
claims.quote: Clips use parent-child relationship rather than chronological order

claims.claim: DaVinci Resolve supports both FCPXML and XMEML, but features that do not exist in the other application will not translate during round-trip interchange.
claims.quote: Some features will not translate back and forth because they don't exist in the other application.

claims.claim: The Ukrainian MFA acoustic model v3.0.0 is installed via the command 'mfa model download acoustic ukrainian_mfa', confirming the exact model name 'ukrainian_mfa'.
claims.quote: mfa model download acoustic ukrainian_mfa

claims.claim: The Ukrainian MFA acoustic model pairs with the 'Ukrainian MFA dictionary v3.0.0' pronunciation dictionary and uses the MFA phone set.

claims.quote: "gmm-hmm" architecture using "MFCC" features

claims.claim: The model was trained on ~192 hours of Ukrainian speech across three corpora: Common Voice Ukrainian v16.1 (94.80 hours), M-AILABS Ukrainian (83.22 hours), and GlobalPhone Ukrainian v3.1 (14.11 hours).
claims.quote: Common Voice Ukrainian v16.1 (94.80 hours, 1,026 speakers, 73,099 utterances)

claims.claim: The model is intended for forced alignment of Ukrainian transcripts, implying a transcript is required as input (e.g. produced by Whisper).
claims.quote: "This model is intended for forced alignment of Ukrainian transcripts."

claims.claim: The DaVinci Resolve Python scripting API can place clips at exact timeline frame positions via the dictionary form of AppendToTimeline, which accepts 'startFrame', 'endFrame', 'recordFrame' (timeline placement position), 'mediaType', and 'trackIndex' keys.
claims.quote: AppendToTimeline([{clipInfo, ...}]) accepting "mediaPoolItem", "startFrame" (int), "endFrame" (int), optional "mediaType" and "trackIndex"

claims.claim: AppendToTimeline appends MediaPoolItem objects to the current timeline and returns the list of appended timelineItems, enabling programmatic placement of many still-image mouth-shape clips.
claims.quote: Appends specified MediaPoolItem objects in the current timeline. Returns the list of appended timelineItems

claims.claim: Resolve can import a timeline from an external interchange file via ImportTimelineFromFile, supporting AAF, EDL, XML, FCPXML, DRT, and ADL formats (including FCPXML 1.8/1.9/1.10).
claims.quote: ImportTimelineFromFile(filePath, {importOptions}) - "Creates timeline based on parameters within given file (AAF/EDL/XML/FCPXML/DRT/ADL)"

claims.claim: Timeline frame rate (which governs seconds-to-frame timecode conversion) is read/set via GetSetting('timelineFrameRate') and SetSetting('timelineFrameRate', x), with drop-frame configurable by appending 'DF'.
claims.quote: GetSetting('timelineFrameRate') and SetSetting('timelineFrameRate', x) - "The property value is one of the frame rates available...Drop Frame can be configured by appending 'DF'"

claims.claim: An empty timeline can be created programmatically with CreateEmptyTimeline(name), and timelines can be built directly from positioned clips with CreateTimelineFromClips(name, [{clipInfo}]).
claims.quote: CreateEmptyTimeline(name) - "Adds new timeline with given name" ... CreateTimelineFromClips(name, [{clipInfo}])

claims.claim: The Resolve Python API's AppendToTimeline accepts a list of clipInfo dicts where each clip specifies mediaPoolItem, startFrame, endFrame, optional mediaType (1=video, 2=audio), trackIndex, and recordFrame — allowing a still-image mouth shape to be placed at an exact frame position on the timeline.
claims.quote: AppendToTimeline([{clipInfo}, ...]) ... Accepts dict with mediaPoolItem, startFrame (float/int), endFrame (float/int), optional mediaType (1=video only, 2=audio only), trackIndex (int), and recordFrame (float/int).

claims.claim: The Resolve API can import timelines from external interchange files via ImportTimelineFromFile, which supports AAF, EDL, XML, FCPXML, DRT, ADL, and OTIO formats.
claims.quote: ImportTimelineFromFile(filePath, {importOptions}) ... Supports AAF/EDL/XML/FCPXML/DRT/ADL/OTIO formats with optional parameters: timelineName, importSourceClips, sourceClipsPath, sourceClipsFolders, interlaceProcessing.

claims.claim: The Resolve Timeline Export method supports multiple interchange export types including EXPORT_FCP_7_XML, EXPORT_FCPXML_1_8/1_9/1_10, EXPORT_OTIO, and EXPORT_DRT.
claims.quote: Export(fileName, exportType, exportSubtype) ... Supports: EXPORT_AAF, EXPORT_EDL, EXPORT_FCP_7_XML, EXPORT_FCPXML_1_8/1_9/1_10, EXPORT_OTIO, EXPORT_DRT, EXPORT_ALE, EXPORT_DOLBY_VISION_VER_2_9/4_0/5_1.

claims.claim: Timeline frame rate is set via the project setting timelineFrameRate, which accepts fractional and drop-frame values such as '23.976' and '29.97 DF', relevant to seconds-to-frame timecode conversion at 24/25/30 fps.
claims.quote: Project:SetSetting('timelineFrameRate', x) Value examples: "29.97 DF" (drop frame), "23.976" (fractional).

claims.claim: CreateTimelineFromClips builds a new timeline from a list of clipInfo dicts each carrying mediaPoolItem, startFrame, endFrame, and recordFrame, enabling programmatic construction of a full viseme image sequence at exact frames.
claims.quote: CreateTimelineFromClips(name, [{clipInfo}]) ... Creates timeline appending clips specified as dict: mediaPoolItem, startFrame, endFrame, recordFrame.

claims.claim: The @chatoctopus/timeline library exports DaVinci Resolve-compatible timelines via the 'resolve' editor target, which emits FCP7 XML / xmeml v5 format (exportTimeline(timeline, "resolve")), confirming xmeml v5 as a valid Resolve import interchange format.
claims.quote: Generates well-formed FCPXML 1.8 (Final Cut Pro), FCP7 XML / xmeml v5 (Premiere, Resolve), and OTIO (OpenTimelineIO) with frame-accurate rational time math -- no floating-point drift.

claims.claim: To place media items at exact frame positions without floating-point drift, the model represents all timing as rational numbers {num, den}; e.g. starting from frame 300 at 29.97 fps is encoded as startTime: rational(300 * 1001, 30000), and 150 frames duration as rational(150 * 1001, 30000).
claims.quote:
startTime: rational(300 * 1001, 30000), // start from frame 300 in source
            duration: rational(150 * 1001, 30000), // 150 frames = ~5 seconds

claims.claim: Still images placed on a timeline require an explicit duration (they have no intrinsic length), which is directly relevant to placing many still-image mouth-shape clips at timed positions.
claims.quote: still images require an explicit `duration`, and mixed-frame-rate trims may be rejected when they cannot be represented consistently

claims.claim: The library provides seconds-to-frame and timecode conversion utilities: secondsToFrameAligned(secs, frameRate) snaps seconds to the nearest frame boundary, toFrames(duration, frameDuration) converts to a frame count, and parseTimecode(tc, frameRate) parses SMPTE timecode including drop-frame support.
claims.quote: `secondsToFrameAligned(secs, frameRate)` | Convert seconds, snapped to nearest frame boundary ... `parseTimecode(tc, frameRate)` | Parse SMPTE timecode (`"01:00:00;00"`) with drop-frame support

claims.claim: Current FCPXML and xmeml (Resolve) exports flatten transitions into butt cuts and do not emit native transition elements, and may drop transitions, markers, and metadata when exporting (a lossy-export limitation), though this is largely irrelevant for placing static mouth-shape stills.
claims.quote: current FCPXML and xmeml exports flatten transitions into butt cuts and warn because those adapters do not yet emit native transition elements

claims.claim: The Ukrainian MFA G2P model uses the MFA Ukrainian phone set with a phone inventory of 87 phones and 34 graphemes, and was trained from the Ukrainian MFA pronunciation dictionaries.
claims.quote: uses the MFA phone set for Ukrainian, and was trained from the pronunciation dictionaries

claims.claim: The model was trained on 58,496 words from the Ukrainian MFA dictionary v3.0.0, linking the G2P model to a companion Ukrainian pronunciation dictionary resource.
claims.quote: Training Corpus: 58,496 words from the Ukrainian MFA dictionary v3.0.0

claims.claim: The Ukrainian MFA G2P model v3.0.0 was released on March 21, 2024, is compatible with MFA version v3.0.0, uses a Phonetisaurus architecture, and is licensed CC BY 4.0.
claims.quote: Release Date: March 21, 2024 ... Architecture: Phonetisaurus ... Compatible MFA Version: v3.0.0 ... License: CC BY 4.0

claims.claim: MFA provides Ukrainian-specific phone set entries, confirming Ukrainian is a supported/trained language in the MFA model ecosystem alongside Russian, Polish, and others.
claims.quote: Covered languages include Arabic, Bulgarian, English (multiple dialects), Hausa, Japanese, Korean, Mandarin, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swahili, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, and Vietnamese.

claims.claim: Ukrainian palatalized velar and alveolar consonants are mapped to palatal consonants in the MFA IPA phone set: kʲ→c, ɦʲ→ç, xʲ→ʝ, nʲ→ɲ, lʲ→ʎ, defining the exact phone symbols a parser will encounter in Ukrainian TextGrid output.
claims.quote: Velar and alveolar soft consonants represented as palatal (kʲ→c, ɦʲ→ç, xʲ→ʝ, nʲ→ɲ, lʲ→ʎ).

claims.claim: Ukrainian alveolar consonants are represented as dental in the MFA phone set except when palatalized, using symbols such as t̪ s̪ t̪s̪ d̪ z̪ d̪z̪ n̪.
claims.quote: Alveolar consonants are dental except when palatalized: "t̪ s̪ t̪s̪ d̪ z̪ d̪z̪ n̪"

claims.claim: The MFA IPA phone set uses a fixed diacritic ordering — (prenasalization) symbol (secondary place) (glottal settings) (length) — which is needed to correctly tokenize multi-character IPA phones when mapping phonemes to visemes.
claims.quote: The system follows a specific sequence: (prenasalization) symbol (secondary place) (glottal settings) (length). Examples include pʰː, pʷʼ, and pʲ̚.

claims.claim: MFA acoustic models are built on this shared IPA phone set as their phonetic foundation, so phone-level TextGrid alignments output IPA symbols rather than ARPAbet.
claims.quote: The acoustic models rely on this IPA phone set as their phonetic foundation.

claims.claim: Rhubarb Lip Sync outputs a Preston Blair-based viseme set of six basic mouth shapes (A-F) plus three optional extended shapes (G, H, X), where A=closed for P/B/M, B=clenched teeth/EE, C=open EH/AE, D=wide open AA, E=rounded AO/ER, F=puckered UW/OW/W, G=teeth-on-lip F/V, H=L, X=idle/rest.
claims.quote: Ⓐ: Closed mouth for "P", "B", "M" sounds ... Ⓖ: Upper teeth touching the lower lip for 'F' and 'V' (optional) ... Ⓧ: Idle/rest position for pauses (optional)

claims.claim: Rhubarb's default English recognizer (PocketSphinx) only recognizes English dialog, so for Ukrainian or other non-English audio one must use the Phonetic recognizer, which is language-independent but usually less precise.
claims.quote: PocketSphinx ... only recognizes English dialog. ... use the Phonetic recognizer, which is language-independent but usually less precise.

claims.claim: Rhubarb supports multiple output formats including TSV, XML, JSON, and DAT (for Moho/OpenToonz), enabling programmatic parsing of timed mouth-shape data.
claims.quote: TSV (tab-separated values), XML, JSON, and DAT formats for Moho/OpenToonz compatibility.

claims.claim: Rhubarb accepts WAVE (.wav) and Ogg Vorbis (.ogg) audio input formats.
claims.quote: Supported formats are "WAVE (.wav) and Ogg Vorbis (.ogg)."

claims.claim: Rhubarb can take an optional plain-text dialog/transcript file to improve recognition accuracy.
claims.quote: specify the path to a plain-text file...containing the dialog contained in the audio file

claims.claim: MFA is installed via conda with the command 'conda create -n aligner -c conda-forge montreal-forced-aligner', and run by activating the environment ('conda activate aligner').
claims.quote: conda create -n aligner -c conda-forge montreal-forced-aligner

claims.claim: MFA requires paired audio and transcript files where the wav filename and its transcript filename must be identical except for the extension; transcripts may be Praat TextGrid, .lab, or .txt files.
claims.quote: The filename of the wav file and its corresponding transcript must be identical except for the extension.

claims.claim: MFA produces aligned TextGrids in the output folder containing both phone-level and word-level timing information; the input corpus needs separate input and output folders, with the output folder empty before running.
claims.quote: Aligned TextGrids are placed in the specified output folder with phone and word-level timing information.

claims.claim: Pretrained MFA acoustic models and pronunciation dictionaries exist for multiple languages, retrievable via 'mfa model download' and listed in the MFA models master list (mfa-models.readthedocs.io); the phone set in the dictionary must match the acoustic model.
claims.quote: The phone set in your dictionary must match that used in the acoustic models and the orthography must match that in the transcripts.

claims.claim: MFA can generate pronunciation dictionaries from text using G2P (grapheme-to-phoneme) models via 'mfa g2p', and the pronunciation dictionary must be a two-column tab-separated text file (word, then space-separated phones).
claims.quote: The pronunciation dictionary must be a two column text file

claims.claim: In the DaVinci Resolve Python scripting API, clips are added to a timeline using the AppendToTimeline() method, and clips are appended in the order they are supplied (one at a time, as a list, or as a Python dictionary).
claims.quote: You're probably using AppendToTimeline() to cut the clips into the timeline. The clips are appended in the order you supply them. This can be one at a time, it can be a list of clips, or it can be a Python dictionary (assuming you're using Python).

claims.claim: The Resolve API gives no built-in switch to sort clips added to a timeline; ordering must be controlled by the script (e.g. sorting filenames or source timecodes before appending), and AppendToTimeline does not guarantee an order when fed an unordered Python dictionary.
claims.quote: Dictionaries in Python 2 are unordered. If you're using a dictionary it's expected the order will be random. It's up to you to either iterate sorted, or order the dictionary keys in the order you require.

claims.claim: A Resolve scripting workflow can programmatically create a new project, build a media pool folder structure, import media from disk, and create a timeline from those clips.
claims.quote: I have a script that creates a new project, builds a pre-defined structure in the media pool , imports media from a disk location, and creates a timeline using those clips.

claims.claim: The user reports the official Resolve scripting documentation does not cover how to control the order in which clips are placed on the timeline.
claims.quote: Is this something that can be controlled? I don't see any info in the documentation.

claims.claim: Amazon Polly maps IPA/X-SAMPA phonemes to a compact set of single-character viseme codes for US English; the consonant set uses codes including p, t, S, T, f, k, i, l, r, s, u and vowels map to codes including @, a, e, E, i, o, O, u (the Oculus/Polly viseme scheme).
claims.quote: The following table lists the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) phonemes, the Extended Speech Assessment Methods Phonetic Alphabet (X-SAMPA) symbols, and the corresponding visemes for the American English voices that are supported by Amazon Polly.

claims.claim: Multiple distinct phonemes collapse to the same viseme: bilabials /b/, /m/, /p/ all map to viseme 'p'; alveolars /d/, /n/, /t/ all map to viseme 't'; velars /g/, /h/, /k/, /ŋ/ all map to viseme 'k'; and postalveolars /dʒ/, /ʃ/, /tʃ/, /ʒ/ all map to viseme 'S'.
claims.quote: b	b	voiced bilabial plosive	bed	p ... m	m	bilabial nasal	mouse	p ... p	p	voiceless bilabial plosive	speak	p

claims.claim: Polly's labiodental fricatives /f/ and /v/ both map to viseme 'f', and the dental fricatives /θ/ and /ð/ both map to viseme 'T', providing a many-to-one phoneme-to-viseme reduction usable for IPA-to-viseme mouth-shape mapping.
claims.quote: f	f	voiceless labiodental fricative	five	f ... v	v	voiced labiodental fricative	vest	f

claims.claim: Stress and boundary symbols (primary stress ˈ, secondary stress ˌ, syllable boundary .) have no associated viseme (blank viseme column), meaning these markers should be ignored when generating mouth shapes.
claims.quote: ˈ	"	primary stress	Alabama

claims.claim: Rhubarb Lip Sync outputs mouth shapes using a set of six basic shapes (A-F) plus three optional extended shapes (G, H, X), based on the Preston Blair viseme convention, where X is the idle/rest position for pauses.
claims.quote: Six basic shapes (A–F) plus three optional extended shapes: Basic: A, B, C, D, E, F; Extended (optional): G, H, X ... Shape X is the "idle position" for pauses in speech.

claims.claim: Rhubarb's default recognizer (PocketSphinx) only recognizes English dialog; a separate language-independent phonetic recognizer mode exists that recognizes individual sounds/syllables rather than whole English words and works across languages but is less precise.
claims.quote: The default PocketSphinx recognizer "only recognizes English dialog." For non-English recordings, the tool is "language-independent" only when using the phonetic mode. ... this recognizer won't try to understand entire (English) words but instead recognize[s] individual sounds and syllables. Results are usually less precise but work across languages.

claims.claim: Rhubarb supports multiple output formats including TSV (most compact), XML (verbose with metadata and mouth cue timing), JSON (similar to XML), and DAT (for Moho/OpenToonz).
claims.quote: TSV (tab-separated values): "simplest and most compact export format"; XML: verbose format with metadata and mouth cue timing; JSON: "very similar to XML format"; DAT: for Moho and OpenToonz animation software

claims.claim: Rhubarb can optionally take a dialog/transcript file via --dialogFile to improve accuracy, preferring words from the transcript but ignoring it where it audibly differs from the recording.
claims.quote: Users can provide text via the --dialogFile option. The tool will "prefer words and phrases that occur in the dialog file," leading to "more reliable animation," though it "will ignore the dialog file where it audibly differs from the recording."

claims.claim: Rhubarb works by analyzing audio, performing speech recognition to determine what sound is being said at what point in time, then automatically generating lip sync timing information.
claims.quote: analyzes your audio files, recognizes what is being said, then automatically generates lip sync information ... what sound is being said at what point in time

claims.claim: Rhubarb Lip Sync 1.x only supports English dialog, and adding other languages (such as Ukrainian) would require rewriting most of its code.
claims.quote: Rhubarb 1.x only supports English dialog. Support for additional languages has often been requested, but due to a number of technical limitations, adding it to Rhubarb 1.x would require a rewrite of most of its code.

claims.claim: Rhubarb uses Preston Blair's mouth shape system consisting of 6 basic shapes plus up to 3 extended shapes.
claims.quote: 6 basic mouth shapes and up to 3 extended mouth shapes, all of which are pre-defined.

claims.claim: Rhubarb 1.x's speech recognition is based on PocketSphinx; the planned Rhubarb 2 will switch to Kaldi as a more modern engine.
claims.quote: For speech recognition, I plan to use use [Kaldi](https://kaldi-asr.org/). It's more modern than PocketSphinx

claims.claim: Rhubarb 2 is planned to be language-agnostic and to use forced alignment of an exact provided dialog transcript rather than a recognition step, including grapheme-to-phoneme via Phonetisaurus.
claims.quote: The architecture of Rhubarb 2 will be language-agnostic from the start. This means that adding more languages should be possible at any time with minimal code changes.

claims.claim: Rhubarb 2's mouth-shape naming is planned to be inspired by Amazon Polly viseme standards, relevant to phoneme-to-viseme mapping schemes.
claims.quote: improved naming conventions inspired by Amazon Polly viseme standards

claims.claim: OVR LipSync uses 15 visemes based on the MPEG-4 standard, named: sil, PP, FF, TH, DD, kk, CH, SS, nn, RR, aa, E, I (ih), O (oh), U (ou).
claims.quote: OVR LipSync uses 15 visemes based on the MPEG-4 standard. The exact viseme names are: sil (silence), PP (p, b, m), FF (f, v), TH (th), DD (t, d), kk (k, g), CH (tS, dZ, S), SS (s, z), nn (n, l), RR (r), aa (A:), E (e), I (ih), O (oh), U (ou)

claims.claim: OVR LipSync maps speech to mouth shapes called visemes, which are a visual analog to phonemes, with each viseme depicting the mouth shape for a specific set of phonemes.
claims.quote: Oculus Lipsync maps human speech to a set of mouth shapes, called 'visemes', which are a visual analog to phonemes. Each viseme depicts the mouth shape for a specific set of phonemes.

claims.claim: Each Oculus viseme groups multiple related phonemes that share similar mouth positions, providing an explicit phoneme-to-viseme grouping (e.g., PP = p/b/m, FF = f/v, CH = tS/dZ/S, SS = s/z, nn = n/l).
claims.quote: Each viseme groups related phonemes that share similar mouth positions. For example, PP combines the phonemes p, b, and m; FF combines f and v; and TH represents the th sound.

claims.claim: The documentation provides three reference images per viseme (mild production, emphasized production, and a 3/4 rotation view) to illustrate the corresponding mouth shapes.
claims.quote: For each viseme, the documentation provides three reference images showing mild production, emphasized production, and a 3/4 rotation view to illustrate the corresponding mouth shapes.

claims.claim: Amazon Polly defines a viseme as the visual equivalent of a phoneme, and provides phoneme-to-viseme mappings using IPA and X-SAMPA symbols, with per-language viseme sets — making it a usable phoneme-to-viseme mapping reference.
claims.quote: A *viseme* represents the position of the face and mouth when saying a word. It is the visual equivalent of a phoneme... The following chart shows a partial list of International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) phonemes and Extended Speech Assessment Methods Phonetic Alphabet (X-SAMPA) symbols as well as their corresponding visemes for US English voices.

claims.claim: Polly's viseme labels are single/short letter codes (e.g. b->p, d->t, d͡ʒ->S, ð->T, f->f, g->k, h->k), grouping multiple phonemes into one viseme because some phonemes look identical when spoken — this is the Oculus-style viseme naming scheme rather than Preston Blair.
claims.quote: not all visemes can be mapped to a particular phoneme because numerous phonemes appear the same when spoken... in English, the words "pet" and "bet" are acoustically different. However, when observed visually (without sound), they look exactly the same.

claims.claim: Polly returns viseme timing data as line-delimited JSON 'speech mark' objects, where each object has a 'time' field giving the timestamp in milliseconds from the start of the audio, a 'type' (including 'viseme'), and a 'value' holding the viseme name — directly usable for converting to frame timecodes.
claims.quote: Amazon Polly returns speech mark objects in a line-delimited JSON stream... **time** – the timestamp in milliseconds from the beginning of the corresponding audio stream... **viseme**: the viseme name

claims.claim: Speech marks (including visemes) are intended for lip-sync use and are available across neural, long-form, and standard TTS engines.
claims.quote: combining the metadata with the audio stream from your text can enable you to synchronize speech with facial animation (lip-syncing)... Speechmarks are available when using neural, long-form, or standard text-to-speech engines.

claims.claim: Each language has its own viseme set tied to its phonemes, and Polly publishes complete per-language phoneme-to-viseme tables — but Ukrainian is not among Polly's supported voices, so Polly cannot directly generate visemes for Ukrainian audio.
claims.quote: Each language has a set of viseme that correspond to their specific phonemes... For the complete table and tables for all available languages, see Languages in Amazon Polly.

claims.claim: Cutlass generates FCPXML files targeting format version 1.13, using <fcpxml version="1.13"> as the root document container.
claims.quote: The tool targets FCPXML 1.13: `<fcpxml version="1.13">` as the root document container.

claims.claim: In FCPXML, still images (PNG/JPG) must be represented as Video elements with duration="0s" assets (formats omitting frameDuration), while videos use AssetClip elements with calculated duration and frameDuration; using the wrong element type crashes FCP.
claims.quote: Images (PNG/JPG): Implemented as `Video` elements with `duration="0s"` assets and formats that omit `frameDuration` ... The guide warns: "Wrong element type = immediate FCP crash"

claims.claim: FCPXML durations use a frame-aligned rational format with a 24000 timebase (e.g. "240240/24000s"), and decimal seconds must never be used because they cause sync drift.
claims.quote: Duration uses frame-aligned rational format: "240240/24000s". The documentation specifies that "FCP timebase: 24000 denominator, 1001 numerator per frame" and emphasizes never using decimal seconds as this "causes sync drift."

claims.claim: Assets are defined with unique IDs (r1, r2, ...), file references via MediaRep using absolute file:// URIs, duration in frame format, and clips reference assets through ref attributes.
claims.quote: Assets are defined with properties including: Unique IDs (`r1`, `r2`, etc.) ... File references via `MediaRep` with absolute file paths: "file:///path/to/video.mp4" ... Clips reference these assets through `ref` attributes.

claims.claim: Cutlass's documentation makes no mention of DaVinci Resolve compatibility; it focuses exclusively on Final Cut Pro.
claims.quote: No mention of DaVinci Resolve compatibility appears in the provided content. The documentation focuses exclusively on Final Cut Pro integration.

claims.claim: FCPXML represents all time values (durations, offsets, start times) as rational numbers in the form numerator/denominator+'s', e.g. frameDuration of '2002/60000s' for 29.97 fps footage and '100/2500s' for 25 fps PAL, because rational time is more accurate than decimal frame rates.
claims.quote: Because it's way more accurate than 30 or even 29.97!

claims.claim: Frame rate in FCPXML is the inverse of the frameDuration value, so a frameDuration of 2002/60000s yields 60000/2002 = 29.97002997 fps; this rational math governs seconds-to-frame timecode conversion.
claims.quote: 60000/2002 = 29.97002997 frames per second

claims.claim: Media files are declared once in a <resources> section with unique id attributes, and clips on the timeline reference them via a ref attribute (e.g. ref="r1") pointing to the corresponding <asset id="r1">.
claims.quote: The asset element gives us information about the media file: the asset-clip element gives us information about the Clip

claims.claim: FCPXML and the legacy FCP7 xmeml format are structurally incompatible: xmeml is track-based while FCPXML has no tracks, instead using a single primary storyline with connected clips, and effects/transitions are unlikely to transfer fully between them.
claims.quote: There are no tracks in FCPX XML

claims.claim: Time spans on clip elements are expressed with start and duration attributes both in rational-seconds form, as in a keyword spanning start="54263209/15000s" duration="33269236/60000s" within an asset-clip referencing ref="r1".
claims.quote: <asset-clip name="clip" ref="r1" duration="399230840/720000s">

claims.claim: In FCP7 xmeml, a clip's timeline position is set by integer 'start' and 'end' subelements of clipitem (frame-based positions), distinct from 'in'/'out' which define media boundaries; start/end exist on clipitem but not on browser clip.
claims.quote: An integer specifying the relative starting point for a clip in a track. ... Note that start, end, link, syncoffset, and enabled are subelements of clipitem, but not of clip.

claims.claim: Still images (jpeg, tiff, PSD) are represented with stillframe=TRUE, and a stillframeoffset integer selects the freeze frame; notably, for still frames the duration value represents minutes rather than frames.
claims.quote: Photoshop PSD files, jpeg files, tiff files, or other still images imported into Final Cut have stillframe set to TRUE. ... Note that in the case of still frames, the value for duration represents minutes rather than frames.

claims.claim: Frame rate is defined by a 'rate' element containing an integer 'timebase' plus a boolean 'ntsc'; setting ntsc=TRUE applies a 0.01% NTSC rate reduction to the timebase (e.g. timebase 30 + ntsc TRUE = 29.97 fps).
claims.quote: An integer specifying the timebase of the frame rate. You use timebase in conjunction with ntsc to define the time scale. ... If you set ntsc to TRUE, the NTSC rate reduction (0.01%) is applied to timebase.

claims.claim: Timecode can be expressed as an integer 'frame' count (an actual frame count, not a time index, accounting for skipped numbers in drop-frame), with displayformat DF/NDF; e.g. string 01:00:2;05 at 29.97 fps equals frame count 107657 in drop frame.
claims.quote: Note that frame contains an actual frame count, not simply a time index. For a drop frame timecode, the skipped numbers are taken into account. ... a string set to 01:00:2;05 with a rate of 29.97 fps translates to a frame count of 107657 formatted using drop frame notation.

claims.claim: Media files are referenced via a 'file' element using a 'pathurl' UTF-8 path that must be on a local volume and must start with file://localhost or file:///, with illegal characters percent-escaped per RFC 2396.
claims.quote: A file referenced by pathurl must be on a local volume. The URL must start with file://localhost or file:///.

claims.claim: steele-fcpxml is a Python library that programmatically generates Final Cut Pro XML (FCPXML) timelines from lists of clip specifications, producing files importable into DaVinci Resolve (or Final Cut Pro).
claims.quote: A Python library for generating Final Cut Pro XML (FCPXML) timelines programmatically, designed to help video editors collaborate with LLMs on automatic edits.

claims.claim: The library uses rational-time arithmetic with frame-rate detection so that timeline offsets do not drift, supporting standard frame rates via a FrameRate enum including 25, 29.97, and 23.976 fps.
claims.quote: emits the correct rational-time arithmetic so timeline offsets never drift

claims.claim: The fluent API exposes add_clip() (with source in/out points), add_gap(), add_marker(), fork(), and write() to assemble and export .fcpxml files; a tc() helper parses MM:SS or HH:MM:SS timecodes into seconds.
claims.quote: add_clip() — position clips with source in/out points ... The tc() function parses timecodes in MM:SS or HH:MM:SS format to seconds.

claims.claim: The library does not explicitly document still-image or image-sequence clip support; only video clips are shown in examples, which is a limitation for placing many still mouth-shape images at exact frame timecodes.
claims.quote: The documentation provided does not explicitly address still image or image sequence support.

claims.claim: steele-fcpxml requires Python 3.12+ and FFmpeg with ffprobe on PATH (for frame-rate detection), is licensed GPLv3+, and is in alpha development status.
claims.quote: Python 3.12+ ... FFmpeg with ffprobe on PATH ... Status: Alpha (Development Status 3) ... License: GPLv3+

BUT the claim's second assertion — that this "is the Oculus-style viseme naming scheme rather than Preston Blair" — is UNSUPPORTED and incorrect. The actual Oculus/OVRLipSync viseme set (per Meta/Oculus docs, developers.meta.com / developer.oculus.com viseme reference) uses 15 names: sil, PP, FF, TH, DD, kk, CH, SS, nn, RR, aa, E, ih, oh, ou. Polly's codes (p, t, S, T, f, k, ...) do NOT match Oculus's codes (PP, DD, TH, kk, ...). AWS docs nowhere state Polly follows Oculus naming. Polly's single/short-character viseme codes are their own convention (an X-SAMPA/Annosoft-style set), not the Oculus scheme. The claim conflates "groups phonemes into shared visemes" (a general property true of both Polly and Oculus) with "uses Oculus naming," which is false. The accurate negative ("not Preston Blair") is correct, but the positive attribution to Oculus is wrong.
